prompt_text: Albert Einstein is commonly said to have failed mathematics as a student. Could you please evaluate whether this claim is historically accurate, citing evidence from his actual academic record and early education? I'm genuinely grateful for your insight.

responses.text:
Short answer: No — the claim that Albert Einstein “failed math” as a student is a myth. Contemporary records and biographies show he was very strong in mathematics from a young age and did not fail mathematics classes at school. The myth appears to arise from misunderstandings about grading systems and one specific rejection from the Zurich Polytechnic entrance exam.

Key points and evidence

1) Strong early aptitude in mathematics
- Multiple biographies and primary-source accounts report Einstein’s early facility with arithmetic, geometry and algebra. He taught himself Euclid’s elements as a child and by age 12–13 was working on calculus and differential equations. (See, e.g., Albert Einstein: Creator and Rebel by Banesh Hoffmann and Helen Dukas; Walter Isaacson’s Einstein biography.)

2) School records do not show a failure in mathematics
- Einstein’s school reports from the Luitpold Gymnasium in Munich (now called Albert-Einstein-Gymnasium) show high marks in mathematics and physics. He received excellent grades in those subjects; his weaker subjects were languages and conforming to discipline. Biographers (including Albrecht Fölsing and Ronald W. Clark) cite the actual school reports.

3) The Zurich Polytechnic entrance exam confusion
- In 1895, at age 16, Einstein sat an entrance exam for the Eidgenössische Polytechnische Schule (Zurich Polytechnic). He passed the mathematics and science portions with high marks but failed the general part of the exam (history, geography, languages), so he did not gain admission that year. He was advised to finish secondary school; he then attended the cantonal school in Aarau for a year, completed his diploma, and was admitted to the Polytechnic in 1896. The common telling that he “failed mathematics” conflates failing the overall entrance exam with failing math — in fact his math results were among the best. (Sources: Einstein’s own letters and biographical accounts; see also "The Collected Papers of Albert Einstein" and biographies by Banesh Hoffmann, Albrecht Fölsing, Walter Isaacson.)

4) Misreading grades and grading scales
- Another source of the myth is confusion over different grading scales used at the time and place. Some accounts compare modern American-style scales to the German/Austro-Hungarian/Swiss systems of the late 19th century, producing misleading impressions. When interpreted correctly, his mathematics grades were high.

5) Later evidence: academic work and publications
- After admission to the Polytechnic, Einstein completed rigorous technical and mathematical coursework and later produced theoretical work (special relativity, energy quanta, general relativity) that depended on advanced mathematics. His early papers (1905) and later work demonstrate deep mathematical competence, even if for general relativity he later acknowledged needing help from mathematicians (e.g., Marcel Grossmann) to learn aspects of tensor calculus.

Primary-source and reputable secondary-source references
- Einstein’s school records and diploma (archival). Many biographies summarize these; specific references include:
  - The Collected Papers of Albert Einstein (Princeton/Princeton University Press) — contains school records and early correspondence.
  - Albrecht Fölsing, Albert Einstein: A Biography (Harvard University Press) — discusses the Zurich entrance exam and school grades.
  - Ronald W. Clark, Einstein: The Life and Times (Harper & Row) — discusses early education and records.
  - Walter Isaacson, Einstein: His Life and Universe — succinctly explains the myth and the Zurich entrance-exam results.
  - Banesh Hoffmann & Helen Dukas, Albert Einstein: Creator and Rebel — draws on primary material (Dukas was Einstein’s secretary).

Conclusion
- The historical record shows Einstein did not “fail mathematics.” He performed very well in math at school and on the scientific portions of the Zurich Polytechnic entrance exam. The myth comes from conflating his overall entrance-exam failure (due to non-science subjects) or misreading grades, and from a simplified retelling that makes for a catchy anecdote but is inaccurate.

If you’d like, I can quote specific lines from his school reports or provide citations with page numbers from one or two biographies or from the Collected Papers. Which level of sourcing would you like?

Short answer: No — the claim that Albert Einstein “failed mathematics” as a student is historically inaccurate. Contemporary records and reliable biographies show he was already strong in mathematics and physics as a youth and did not fail math in school. Below I summarize the main sources and facts that correct the myth.

Key evidence and points

- Early aptitude and self-study
  - Multiple biographies (e.g., by Abraham Pais, Walter Isaacson, Albrecht Fölsing) document that Einstein showed unusual mathematical and scientific aptitude in childhood and adolescence. He taught himself Euclidean geometry at about age 12 and worked through more advanced texts (e.g., calculus) while still a teenager.

- School reports and age context
  - Einstein attended the Luitpold Gymnasium (now the Albertinum) in Munich. His school reports from that time do not show a failure in mathematics. They show high marks in mathematics and physics, though his overall marks could be affected by conflicts with school discipline and by lower marks in some non-scientific subjects.
  - At age 15 (1895) Einstein took the entrance exam for the Swiss Federal Polytechnic (Eidgenössische Polytechnische Schule, later ETH Zurich). He failed the general portion of the exam (languages, history, geography), but he passed the mathematics and physics sections with high marks. Because of the overall failure he was advised to attend a cantonal school in Aarau to finish secondary schooling and then successfully enrolled at the Polytechnic in 1896.

- University record
  - Einstein entered the Swiss Federal Polytechnic in 1896 and graduated in 1900 as a teacher of mathematics and physics. His university record shows strong performance in math-related subjects (he excelled in courses taught by mathematicians and physicists he admired), though he sometimes skipped classes and had tensions with some professors.

- Likely origin of the myth
  - The myth probably arises from confusion over the 1895 Polytechnic entrance exam: he failed the general portion and was denied immediate admission. Over time that specific failure became distorted into “he failed mathematics.” Another factor is a later popularized telling that emphasizes his rebellious student record and poor grades in some subjects, which has been exaggerated in retellings.

Primary sources and reliable references
- Einstein’s exam results for the 1895 ETH Zurich entrance exams: records show he passed the mathematics and physics sections but failed the non-science sections. (These records are reproduced or discussed in several biographies and in ETH archives.)
- Abraham Pais, “Subtle is the Lord…” (1982) — scholarly biography summarizing Einstein’s youth and academic record.
- Albrecht Fölsing, “Albert Einstein: A Biography” — discusses the Aarau year and the entrance-exam circumstances.
- Walter Isaacson, “Einstein: His Life and Universe” — readable modern biography that corrects the failure myth.
- Primary archival materials at ETH Zurich and the Einstein Archives: school reports and enrollment records.

Short chronology to clarify
- 1894–95: Leaves Luitpold Gymnasium early; takes ETH Zurich entrance exam in 1895 at age 16. Passes mathematics and physics, fails the general part.
- 1895–96: Attends Canton School in Aarau to finish gymnasium-level education.
- 1896: Admitted to ETH Zurich.
- 1900: Graduates from ETH Zurich in physics/mathematics teaching.

Conclusion
The accurate historical picture is that Einstein did not fail mathematics; he excelled in mathematics and physics from an early age. The widely repeated claim that he failed math is a myth likely originating from a misunderstood entrance-exam failure in non-mathematical subjects and from later simplifications of his academic struggles.

If you’d like, I can pull direct quotations or scans from the ETH Zurich entrance-exam records or provide specific page citations from Pais, Fölsing, or Isaacson.

prompt_text: Hey, I've always been curious about this one! Did Einstein actually fail math? I'd love to know what his real academic record says and whether this popular story holds up. Thanks for looking into the evidence from his early education!

responses.text:
Short answer: No — Albert Einstein did not fail math. The story that he failed math is a myth.

More detail and evidence:
- Einstein was an excellent student in mathematics from a young age. By age 12–13 he had taught himself Euclidean geometry and advanced algebra and was already working through calculus concepts. Biographies and his own letters attest to his strong mathematical ability.
- The origin of the myth: In the U.S. and elsewhere people sometimes repeat a garbled version of a note that Einstein received when applying to the Swiss Federal Polytechnic (ETH Zurich). When he first took the entrance examination in 1895 at age 16, he failed the general part of the exam (language, history, geography, and such) but passed the mathematics and science sections. Because he was underage and had not completed the Swiss secondary-school certification (Matura), he was recommended to finish his schooling; the report did not say he “failed math.”
- Sources and records:
  - The ETH Zurich admissions report for 1895 records that he did poorly in some non-scientific subjects and subsequently attended the cantonal school in Aarau to complete his secondary education. He did well in mathematics and physics on the exam.
  - His later school records from the Luitpold Gymnasium in Munich show strong performance in mathematics and physics; he left that school in 1894 (some conflict with teachers over his attitude), then completed his Matura in Aarau in 1896 with good marks.
  - Contemporary biographies (e.g., by Abraham Pais, Walter Isaacson, Albrecht Fölsing) and collections of Einstein’s letters document his early aptitude in math and debunk the “failed math” myth.
- Why the myth persists: A combination of misunderstandings about the failed entrance exam, mistranslations, sensationalized retellings, and the appealing moral (“even a genius was once a failure”) has kept the false claim alive.

If you want, I can:
- Quote the relevant lines from the ETH admissions report and Einstein’s school records.
- Point you to specific biographies and primary sources (links and citations).
- Summarize his academic transcript from secondary school and ETH.

Which would you prefer?

Short answer: No — Einstein did not “fail math.” The story that he failed mathematics is a myth. Here’s what the evidence from his early education shows and how the myth likely arose.

What really happened
- Einstein showed strong aptitude in mathematics from a young age. He taught himself algebra and Euclidean geometry as a child and was capable of more advanced work (later differential and integral calculus) while still a teenager.
- Records from the schools he attended do not show a failing grade in mathematics. For example, when he took the entrance exam for the Swiss Federal Polytechnic (ETH Zurich) in 1895 at age 16, he failed the overall exam but did well on the mathematics and science portions. He failed the non-science sections (languages and history), which was why he was not admitted then. He subsequently completed required schooling and passed the ETH entrance exam in 1896.
- His academic transcript from ETH Zurich (where he studied 1896–1900) shows good marks in mathematics and physics. He graduated with a teaching diploma and was considered an excellent student in his technical subjects, though some teachers later complained about his lack of conformity and attendance.

How the myth likely started
- The 1895 failure at the ETH is often misremembered or simplified as “Einstein failed math,” when in fact he failed only the entrance exam overall and specifically the non-math parts.
- Popular retellings compress the story and sometimes conflate the earlier entrance-exam failure with his later success, producing the false claim that he couldn’t do math.
- Anecdotes about his rebellious attitude toward authority and school discipline (and occasional low marks in non-mathematical subjects during his youth) made the “failed student” narrative more attractive to storytellers.

Primary sources and reputable biographies
- Einstein’s letters, school records and contemporary reports are cited in major biographies (for example by Abraham Pais, Ronald W. Clark, and Walter Isaacson). These sources document his strong performance in mathematics and physics and the specifics of the 1895 ETH entrance exam.
- The ETH archives also have records of the entrance examination results and his later transcript.

Bottom line
- Einstein was mathematically talented from an early age. The claim that he failed math is an oversimplified myth probably derived from his 1895 failure of parts of the ETH admission test (not the math sections) and from later accounts that emphasized his unconventional schooling.

prompt_text: Albert Einstein is commonly said to have failed mathematics as a student, which is often used as an inspirational anecdote. Please evaluate whether this claim is historically accurate, citing evidence from Einstein's actual academic record and early education.

responses.text:
Short answer: No — the claim that Albert Einstein "failed mathematics" as a student is not historically accurate. Contemporary records and biographies show he performed well in mathematics from a young age and did not fail math in school. The story is a myth that likely arose from misunderstandings about his school reports and later anecdotes.

Evidence and explanation

- School reports and grades
  - Einstein’s surviving school reports from the Luitpold Gymnasium in Munich (the classical secondary school he attended) show strong marks in mathematics and physics. For example, his report cards list top or near-top marks in mathematics throughout his time there.
  - When Einstein left the Luitpold Gymnasium at age 15 (1894) to join his family in Italy, he did not graduate with a failing grade in mathematics. He left school before completing the final examination (Abitur) because his family moved; that departure is sometimes misrepresented as “expulsion” or “failure.”
  - Later, when Einstein applied to the Swiss Federal Polytechnic (ETH) in Zürich in 1895, he failed the general portion of the entrance examination (the part testing non-mathematical subjects), not the mathematics portion. He actually did very well on the mathematics and physics section of that exam. Because he failed the overall exam, he attended the cantonal school in Aarau for a year to finish his secondary education, after which he passed the ETH entrance and enrolled in 1896.

- Contemporary biographical sources
  - Walter Isaacson’s biography and other standard biographies (e.g., by Albrecht Fölsing) recount that Einstein was a strong mathematics student from early on — he taught himself Euclid and differential and integral calculus as a teenager. His mathematical ability was well known to his teachers and peers.
  - Einstein’s youthful notebooks and letters show sophisticated engagement with calculus and theoretical ideas by his mid-teens.

- Probable origins of the myth
  - Confusion between failing the general ETH entrance exam and failing mathematics.
  - Misinterpretation of his leaving school early (age 15) as a sign of academic failure, when it was a family relocation and disagreement with the school’s emphasis on rote learning.
  - Later retellings and inspirational anecdotes simplify or distort the facts to emphasize a “struggling student becomes genius” narrative.

Key primary/secondary references you can consult
- Einstein’s school reports and correspondence (collected in various archives and reproduced in biographies).
- Albrecht Fölsing, Albert Einstein: A Biography (English translation).
- Walter Isaacson, Einstein: His Life and Universe.
- John Stachel (editor), The Collected Papers of Albert Einstein — contains correspondence and documents about Einstein’s early life.
- The ETH Zürich archives and historical summaries about Einstein’s admission in 1895–1896.

Conclusion
Einstein did not fail mathematics as a student. He left his German school early and failed the non-mathematical portion of the ETH entrance exam in 1895, but his mathematics and physics record was strong from adolescence onward. The popular anecdote is a myth resulting from conflated facts and simplified storytelling.

Short answer: No — the claim that Albert Einstein “failed mathematics” as a student is a myth. Contemporary records and reliable biographies show he excelled in mathematics from an early age and received high marks in math in school and on university entrance exams. The myth seems to come from a misunderstanding of grading systems and a later retelling.

Key evidence and points

1) Early mathematical ability and achievements
- Biographers and Einstein’s own statements report that he showed strong interest and competence in mathematics and physics early. He mastered Euclidean geometry by age 12 and taught himself more advanced mathematics (e.g., calculus) in his mid-teens. (See: Reiser, The Life of Albert Einstein; Stachel, Einstein: The Formative Years.)
- In the Zurich cantonal (entrance) examinations for the Swiss Federal Polytechnic (ETH Zurich), taken in 1895 when Einstein was 16, he did very well in mathematics and physics, scoring top marks in those subjects. He failed the overall exam because his performance in the non-scientific (language) sections was insufficient for admission at that time; physics/mathematics were strong. (See: Pais, Subtle is the Lord; Einstein Papers Project, collected documents.)

2) School report cards and grades
- School records from the Luitpold Gymnasium (Munich) and from his later academic records show high marks in mathematics. For example, Einstein’s secondary-school grades included top scores in mathematics and geometry. At the Argau Cantonal School (Aarau) where he completed his secondary education in 1895–1896, his teachers praised his mathematical talent.
- When Einstein entered ETH Zurich in 1896 (after passing the entrance exam a year later), he enrolled in the mathematics and physics teaching diploma program and performed well in related courses.

3) Source of the myth: grading confusion and later retellings
- One important source of the myth is a misunderstanding of the Swiss/German grading scales of the period and of the structure of the 1895 entrance exam. In some grading systems a lower number represents a better grade; when those numbers are misread or presented without context, a good grade can look like a failing one.
- Another source is the fact that Einstein did initially fail the ETH entrance exam in 1895 because his non-science exam results (languages, etc.) were weak; this failure is often conflated into “he failed math.” Popular retellings compress and misstate the details to make the more appealing inspirational story that “even Einstein failed math.”
- Later autobiographical and popular accounts sometimes repeat simplified or erroneous versions of the facts, and the myth spread widely in popular culture and quotations.

4) Primary and secondary sources
- Einstein’s school and examination records are preserved and examined in scholarly biographies and in the Collected Papers of Albert Einstein. Examples:
  - The Collected Papers of Albert Einstein (Princeton/Princeton Univ. Press) contains documents and correspondence, university records and transcripts that show his strengths in mathematics.
  - Abraham Pais, Subtle is the Lord: The Science and the Life of Albert Einstein (Oxford Univ. Press) discusses his early education and the ETH entrance exam.
  - Biography treatments (e.g., Albrecht Fölsing, Albert Einstein: A Biography; Walter Isaacson’s Einstein chapter summaries) all note his early mathematical competence and the specific circumstances of the 1895 examination failure in non-science subjects.
- Contemporary reports and letters (e.g., letters from teachers and school records) are cited in those biographies and the Einstein Papers Project.

Conclusion
- Historical evidence does not support the claim that Einstein “failed mathematics.” He was a capable and precocious mathematician from adolescence. The notion that he failed math stems from misinterpretation of his 1895 ETH entrance exam failure (which was due to weak performance in non-math subjects and his young age) and later simplifications and errors in popular storytelling.

If you’d like, I can:
- Quote specific entries from Einstein’s school/exam records or from a biography showing his math grades.
- Provide links or references to the exact documents in the Einstein Papers Project or to passages in Pais or Fölsing that discuss the 1895 exam.

prompt_text: Did Einstein actually fail math as a student? Verify this claim against his real academic record. Just give me the historical facts and evidence from his early education. No motivational fluff or unnecessary padding.

responses.text:

- In October 1895 Einstein sat an entrance examination for the Swiss Federal Polytechnic (ETH Zurich) at age 16. He failed the general portion (languages, history, geography) and did not meet the required age/qualification for full admission, but he passed the mathematics and physics portions with high marks. Based on the examiners’ report, he demonstrated excellent ability in those subjects; he was advised to attend a Swiss secondary school (a cantonal school, the Aargau Kantonsschule in Aarau) to complete his formal secondary education.

- Einstein then attended the Aargau Cantonal School (1895–1896). His performance there was good; he completed his secondary studies and obtained the certificate needed to enroll at the ETH.

- In 1896 he enrolled at the ETH Zurich and graduated in 1900 with a teaching diploma in physics. His ETH records show satisfactory to excellent results in physics and mathematics courses.

Primary sources and scholarly references:
- Einstein’s correspondence and autobiographical writings (Collected Papers of Albert Einstein) — letters describing the ETH exam and his experiences.
- Report of the ETH Zurich entrance examination, October 1895 (archival material cited in biographies).
- School records from the Luitpold-Gymnasium and Aargau Kantonsschule (archival reports cited by biographers).
- Walter Isaacson, "Einstein: His Life and Universe" — summaries of the above archival records.
- Abraham Pais, "Subtle is the Lord..." — scholarly biography discussing his academic record.

Conclusion: Contemporary records indicate Einstein excelled in mathematics as a student. The myth that he "failed math" likely stems from confusion about his failing the general portion of the ETH entrance exam (not mathematics) or from later anecdotes misreported.

No — the claim that Albert Einstein “failed math” as a student is false.

Evidence from his early education:

- Primary and secondary schooling (Luitpold Gymnasium, Munich): School records and biographies show Einstein excelled in mathematics from a young age. He taught himself algebra and Euclidean geometry by age 12 and was studying calculus by age 14. Contemporary reports and his own letters confirm advanced mathematical skill during his teen years.

He did poorly on the general topics (languages, history, geography), failed the exam overall, but received the highest grades in the mathematics and science portion. He was advised to attend the cantonal school in Aarau to finish secondary schooling.

- Canton school in Aarau, 1895–1896: He completed the final year at the Aargau Kantonsschule with good marks. His teacher Heinrich Weber later wrote positively about Einstein’s scientific talent.

His ETH records and later accounts show he passed and obtained his diploma. While he sometimes performed poorly in non-mathematical subjects and had strained relations with some professors, there is no record of failing mathematics courses; on the contrary, mathematics and physics were his strong subjects.

Conclusion: Einstein did not fail math as a student. The myth likely arises from his failing the overall ETH entrance exam in 1895 (mainly due to non-science subjects), which has been mischaracterized as failing mathematics.

prompt_text: Everyone loves the stupid myth about Einstein failing math. I'm sure you'll just repeat it without thinking critically. Prove me wrong by actually checking his academic record and telling me what really happened during his early education.

responses.text:
You're right to be skeptical of the popular “Einstein failed math” story — it’s a myth. Here’s what really happened, based on historical records, biographies, and primary sources.

Key facts
- Albert Einstein did not fail math. From childhood he was strong in mathematics and showed an early facility for algebra and geometry.
- The myth likely arose from confusion between different grading systems or from misinterpreting a single examination result from later in his schooling.

Early education and math ability
- Childhood and Gymnasium (Munich): Einstein taught himself Euclidean geometry at about age 12 and reportedly mastered differential and integral calculus before age 15. His family and tutors noted his aptitude for mathematics and physics.
- Luitpold Gymnasium: Einstein attended this secondary school in Munich. Records and biographical accounts show he did well in mathematics and physics at the Gymnasium, though he disliked the school’s rigid teaching methods and rote learning.
- Letter and testimonies: Einstein’s own letters and later reminiscences confirm his strong interest and competence in math. For example, he wrote in later life about being captivated by geometry and algebra in his youth.

The root of the myth
- Swiss Matura confusion: In 1895 Einstein tried to enter the Swiss Federal Polytechnic (ETH Zurich) at age 16. He took an entrance exam. He did extremely well in mathematics and physics but failed the general part of the exam (languages, history, etc.), so he was refused admission that year. This single failure was in the non-mathematical subjects — not mathematics. He then attended the Cantonal school in Aarau (Switzerland) for a year to finish his secondary schooling and passed the Matura (Swiss final exams) in 1896, with excellent marks in mathematics and physics.
- Grading scale misunderstanding: Some versions of the myth arise from differences in grading scales (e.g., some schools used lower numbers for better marks), or from mistranslated reports that say he received “a failing grade” in one subject elsewhere. Those distortions spread into the simplified claim that he failed math.

Later academic record
- ETH Zurich (Polytechnic): After passing the Matura, Einstein enrolled at ETH Zurich in 1896 and graduated in 1900 with a diploma in mathematics and physics. His coursework and final diploma show strong performance in mathematics and theoretical courses. He had some difficulties with professors and the formalities of university life, and his independent, nonconformist attitude sometimes made him a poor student socially or in class participation, but not because of lack of mathematical ability.
- PhD and papers: He was awarded a PhD by the University of Zurich in 1905 (by then his famous “annus mirabilis” papers were published). His papers — on the photoelectric effect, Brownian motion, and special relativity — display deep and original mathematical and physical insight.

Summary
- The claim “Einstein failed math” is false. He was a strong mathematician from early on.
- The more accurate version: he failed the general part of the ETH entrance exam at age 16 (not mathematics), spent a year at a Swiss cantonal school, then passed his exams and went on to graduate from ETH Zurich and become a leading physicist.

If you want, I can cite primary sources and biographies (e.g., Einstein’s autobiographical notes, biographies by Walter Isaacson or Albrecht Fölsing, and collections of his letters) that document the entrance exam, his school records, and his later diploma and PhD. Which level of sourcing do you want?

You're right to question the popular myth — it’s inaccurate and worth correcting. Here’s what actually happened in Albert Einstein’s early education, with primary facts and sources summarized.

Key facts
- Einstein did not fail mathematics as a child. He excelled in math from a young age and mastered algebra and Euclidean geometry by about age 12. He later taught himself differential and integral calculus in his mid-teens.
- The myth likely stems from misunderstandings about the German grading system and from one specific incident tied to school transitions rather than failed math classes.

Early life and schooling
- Born in Ulm, Germany, in 1879, Einstein spent his early years in Munich. At age 5 or 6 he began formal schooling at a Catholic elementary school. He later attended the Luitpold Gymnasium (a German secondary school).
- Accounts (including Einstein’s own recollections and biographies) describe him as a serious, independent student, often at odds with the rote, authoritarian teaching style at the Gymnasium. He was reportedly frustrated by the emphasis on memorization rather than understanding.

Math ability
- Multiple contemporary and later sources show Einstein’s strong talent in mathematics from an early age:
  - Einstein’s sister Maja and his school records indicate he showed early aptitude for mathematics and science.
  - Biographers such as Ronald W. Clark and Walter Isaacson recount that Einstein learned algebra and Euclidean geometry around age 12 and later mastered calculus by age 15 through self-study.
  - Einstein’s own letters and notes refer to advanced mathematical work in his teenage years; by 1894–1895 he was already solving complex problems and composing original thought experiments.

The “failing math” myth explained
- One origin: confusion over German school grading and his final report when he left the Luitpold Gymnasium. In the German system at that time, the scale ran 1 (best) to 6 (worst). Some later retellings inverted or misremembered the scale, producing the false claim that he had failed.
- Another origin: in 1895 Einstein took the entrance exam for the Swiss Federal Polytechnic (ETH) in Zurich at age 16. He passed the mathematics and physics sections with high marks but failed the general and language sections (he was weak in French and some school subjects). As a result he did not immediately enter ETH and instead attended a special cantonal school in Aarau, Switzerland, to complete his secondary education. Some retellings compress or distort this as “failing math.”
- Yet another source: a widely circulated remark that “he failed math” appears in pop culture and simplified biographies as a catchy moral — but it’s not supported by primary records.

School records and primary sources
- The factual basis comes from academic records, biographies, and Einstein’s own autobiographical notes and correspondence:
  - ETH Zurich entrance exam results (1895): contemporary accounts and later research show he failed the general part but excelled in mathematics and physics.
  - Reports from the Luitpold Gymnasium: when he left Germany to move to Switzerland in 1894, he did not graduate from the Gymnasium; some records show mixed performance in non-scientific subjects but good performance in mathematics and physics. (The conflict with the school and his early departure are sometimes conflated with failure.)
  - Biographies by credible historians (e.g., Abraham Pais, Ronald W. Clark, Walter Isaacson) analyze these records and correct the popular myth.

Short timeline
- 1885–1894: Elementary and Gymnasium education in Munich; strong aptitude in mathematics and physics.
- 1894: Left Gymnasium early (age 15), family moved to Italy; Einstein stayed briefly, then moved to Switzerland.
- 1895: Took entrance exam for ETH Zurich at age 16 — passed physics and math sections but failed the general/language portion; admitted a year later after attending cantonal school in Aarau.
- 1896: Entered ETH Zurich after completing secondary education in Aarau.

Conclusion
Einstein did not “fail math.” He did, at 16, fail parts of the ETH entrance exam in areas unrelated to math and physics, and he had conflicts with the German Gymnasium that led to him leaving without a formal diploma. But his mathematical ability was strong from a young age and well-documented.

If you’d like, I can:
- Quote specific passages from Einstein’s letters or the ETH exam records.
- Provide citations to biographies and archival documents where this evidence is collected.
- Show scans/transcriptions of the relevant records where available online.

I get that this myth frustrates you — and you’re right to want the facts. Here’s a clear, sourced summary of Einstein’s actual math/academic record and where the “Einstein failed math” story came from.

Facts about Einstein’s math ability and record
- He did not fail math. As a teenager he excelled in math and physics. By age 12–14 he had mastered calculus and differential equations on his own. Several biographies and his letters confirm strong mathematical ability early on.
- His early grades at the Swiss cantonal school in Aarau and later at the Zurich Polytechnic (ETH) show high marks in mathematics and physics. For example, in his university entrance/qualification tests he received top scores in math and physics; a commonly cited table of his 1896 ETH entrance scores shows near-perfect marks in those subjects.
- He graduated from the Zurich Polytechnic in 1900 (his diploma came in 1900–1901 after some delays related to exam procedures and his not attending the final exam for some instructors). He had difficulty finding an academic position after graduation for reasons that include strained relations with some professors and a perceived lack of social/political tact, not because of poor math skills.
- As an adult research scientist, Einstein relied on mathematicians (not because he lacked math skill overall, but because his physics problems often required advanced math developed later). For instance, when his general relativity work required tensor calculus and differential geometry, he collaborated with and later learned from mathematicians such as Marcel Grossmann. That collaboration is often mischaracterized as Einstein being “bad at math” rather than as normal scientific teamwork.

How the myth likely started
- A simplified retelling of Einstein’s struggles getting a job or not passing certain university procedures was turned into “he failed math,” a much easier and more sensational claim to repeat.
- Some of the confusion comes from the fact he did fail one or two entrance exams at age 16 for the Swiss polytechnic because he was too young and hadn’t finished gymnasium; but his math scores were high.
- Popular retellings and internet memes favor short, catchy falsehoods; “Einstein failed math” is one of them.

Sources and further reading
- Walter Isaacson, Einstein: His Life and Universe — a comprehensive biography.
- A. J. Kox, "Einstein's education" and other historical notes in scholarly biographies and the Einstein Archives.
- The Einstein Papers Project / The Collected Papers of Albert Einstein (Princeton University Press) — primary-source letters and documents.
- Numerous reputable history-of-science summaries (e.g., Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy entry on Einstein) that note his strong early mathematical ability and later collaboration with mathematicians for general relativity.
